title: Monotone Multi-Horizon Hazard Stack With Beta-Calibrated Blend
summary: Recent F1 pit-stop papers report value from jointly predicting next-stop timing and classification, and discrete-time hazard models are a natural fit for lap-level event prediction.
rationale: Recent F1 pit-stop papers report value from jointly predicting next-stop timing and classification, and discrete-time hazard models are a natural fit for lap-level event prediction. A practical variant here is to predict pit within 1/2/3/5 laps jointly, enforce monotone horizon consistency in post-processing, then derive the next-lap probability from the horizon stack. Beta calibration is safer than isotonic when you want probability correction without unnecessary rank damage.
implementation_hint: Train shared-feature heads for targets like pit<=1, <=2, <=3, <=5 laps or one binary model plus auxiliary remaining-laps-to-pit regression. Convert cumulative horizon outputs to a consistent next-lap hazard, then apply OOF beta or sigmoid calibration to the final blend rather than isotonic.
expected_effect: Should smooth noisy boundary cases near pit windows, improve probability shape, and add model diversity beyond single-head binary boosting.
risk: More moving parts; if the auxiliary heads are weak or inconsistently calibrated, the derived next-lap hazard can become less sharp than a plain binary model.
